title: Spike raster: event times by identified sender and trial

purpose.canonicalQuestion: At what times, and from which identified senders and trials, were events recorded inside a declared window — with every event preserved, and with every recorded sender and every declared trial keeping its row even when nothing happened on it?
cannotEstablish: A firing rate. Apparent tick density depends on mark width, row height, and the pixels available: at 800 px across a 1000 ms window one pixel column spans 1.25 ms, so ticks saturate long before the rate does. Use neuro.population_rate.

Synchrony. Two ticks that look vertically aligned may be a whole pixel column apart — 1.25 ms at the geometry above, which is longer than many monosynaptic delays. Alignment in a raster is a fact about the display, not about the spikes.

That an empty row is a silent neuron. It shows only that no event was RECORDED for that sender in that window. Cortexel can verify that the declared universe is internally consistent; it cannot verify that the recorder saw every spike.
Causality or propagation. A left-to-right sequence of ticks across rows is an ordering of observations, not evidence that one neuron drove another.
That two neighbouring rows are related. Row order is a declared display order; vertical proximity carries no measured similarity, distance, or connectivity.
Correlation structure. The eye reliably invents structure in a raster that a shuffle control removes. Use neuro.correlogram, which has a denominator.
That the recorded senders are representative of any larger population.
The exact number of events at one instant, read from the image: ticks closer than one device pixel are drawn on top of each other. Only the table is exact.

normalization: Recorder output is NOT assumed chronological. NEST emits per-thread and per-rank buffers, and a Neo object may arrive unsorted. Events are stably sorted by (time, rowIndex, original ordinal) for draw order and table order only; the original array index is preserved for every event and is a table column, so any row can be traced back to the caller's input.

The observation window is half-open [start, stop) by default. An event exactly at `stop` is OUT. Under `outOfWindowPolicy: reject` that is an error; under `exclude_and_disclose` the event is excluded, counted, disclosed, and still listed in the table with `inWindow = false`. It is never deleted, because a deleted event at the window edge is exactly what makes a train look as if it stopped.

Duplicate identical event rows are PRESERVED and drawn as two events. Cortexel never deduplicates: two recorder rows for the same spike is a question about the source data, and the renderer is not entitled to answer it by silently removing one.

Sender, trial, and population ids are opaque strings. A numeric source id is normalized to its canonical decimal string; no id is coerced from an arbitrary label and none is zero-padded. `007` and `7` are different senders and are never merged.

Event times are never null. A null is a missing observation, and a missing observation is not an event — there is nothing at a location to draw and nothing to count. Negative zero is ACCEPTED: RFC 8785 canonicalizes it to `0`, so -0 and 0 are the same instant and the digest is unchanged. There is deliberately no error code for it.

Unit conversion happens only inside the time dimension and only through the typed unit layer. `eventTimes.unit` and `window.unit` must share the time dimension; every conversion is recorded in the derivation and disclosed.

Populations are exactly the distinct values of `senderPopulationIds`, and their block order is first appearance in `recordedSenderIds`. There is no separate population list, so a population id cannot dangle and cannot fall out of sync with the rows.

Row order is never derived from an observed statistic. Sorting rows by firing rate, first-spike latency, or event count produces a smooth diagonal band that reads as population structure and is entirely an artifact of the sort. If you want that order, put it in `recordedSenderIds` so it is auditable as a choice you made.
derivation: Below the mark budget a raster performs NO aggregation: each accepted event becomes exactly one mark at (time, rowIndex). The invariant is count preservation — drawnMarkCount == acceptedEventCount — and it is asserted in the artifact rather than assumed.

Row assignment: the row key is `senderId`, or (`senderId`, `trialId`) when trials are declared. Rows are the full declared product of `recordedSenderIds` x `trialIds`. A sender that never fired and a trial in which nothing happened each keep their row: that is the one thing a raster shows which a rate curve cannot, and it is destroyed the moment rows are inferred from the events.

Row order: `as_declared` uses the order of `recordedSenderIds`; `canonical_sender_id` sorts by the canonical id string in Unicode code-point order; `grouped_by_population` emits population blocks in first-appearance order and orders senders inside a block by `recordedSenderIds`. Trials are always ordered by the declared `trialIds` order within a sender. Every tie-break is total and deterministic.

Density compaction (`raster_density_bins`), only above the mark budget: an explicit timeBins x rowBins grid over the window and the row axis. Tiles are half-open with the final edge inclusive, so every accepted event lands in exactly one tile. Tile counts are exact integers summing to acceptedEventCount — nothing is dropped — and the grid dimensions and before/after counts are recorded.

Row merging inside the density grid merges only CONTIGUOUS blocks of the final row order. Rows are never reordered to make a tile denser, and a tile that spans several senders is labelled as spanning them: its count cannot be attributed to one neuron.

Density grid dimensions, when the caller declares none: timeBins = clamp(floor(plotWidthPx / 2), 1, 4096) and rowBins = min(rowCount, clamp(floor(plotHeightPx / 2), 1, 4096)), where the plot extent is a deterministic function of `presentation.width` / `presentation.height` and the renderer revision. The chosen dimensions are always recorded and named in the disclosure.

Window and tile membership is decided on the canonicalized binary64 value with a bounded allowance of a few ulp at the edge. The allowance is a constant of the edge, never a function of the bin index: a tolerance that grows with the index would move real sub-boundary events into the wrong tile.

Window VALIDITY is a precondition of window MEMBERSHIP: `window.valid` is evaluated first, and a degenerate or inverted window fails with SCIENCE_WINDOW_INVALID alone. `events.within_window` does not also run, because an event cannot be reported as outside a bound that is not a bound — that would return one error per event for a single authoring mistake.

Complexity: O(n log n) in events for the stable sort, O(n) for mark emission, O(n + timeBins * rowBins) for the density grid, and O(|recordedSenderIds| * |trialIds|) for row allocation, which is preflighted against the budget before any row is materialized.
scopeRules: `recordedSenderIds` is declared, non-empty, and unique. It is the ROW universe, not the set of senders that happened to fire.
Every event's sender is a member of `recordedSenderIds`: an event from a sender that was supposedly not recorded is a contradiction in the source, not a value to include quietly.

When `eventTrialIds` are supplied, the complete trial universe `trialIds` is declared and every event's trial belongs to it. A trial with no events still occupies its row — inferring the trial set from the observed trial ids deletes exactly the trials that carry the strongest negative evidence.

`eventCompleteness` asserts that the supplied events are complete for the declared senders inside the window. A sampled event set is refused, not disclosed: if ticks are a sample, every gap becomes ambiguous between `did not fire` and `was not sampled`, and the figure can no longer be read at all.

`senderUniverseComplete` declares whether the rows are the complete recording or a declared selection from a larger one. Either way the raster makes no population-level claim; the flag is carried into the artifact, the summary, and the table metadata as the completeness label.

Above the mark budget, `aboveMarkBudget: refuse` fails with RESOURCE_MARKS_EXCEEDED rather than compacting. Cortexel never silently truncates and never prefix-slices a raster: dropping later events would erase the end of every train.

eventTimes.description: One time per recorded event, in the caller's declared time unit.

values.description: Finite binary64 times. Never null: a missing observation is not an event, so there is nothing to place and nothing to count.

eventSenderIds.description: The sender of each event, parallel to eventTimes.values. Every value must be a member of recordedSenderIds.

eventTrialIds.description: Optional. The trial of each event, parallel to eventTimes.values. When present, the complete trial universe `trialIds` is required and rows become (sender, trial) pairs.

eventIds.description: Optional stable per-event identity, parallel to eventTimes.values. When absent, a deterministic ordinal identity is assigned from the original row index and the assignment method is recorded, so an event can always be pointed at even though it was not named.

recordedSenderIds.description: The COMPLETE set of senders that were recorded, INCLUDING every sender that never fired. These are the rows. It cannot be inferred from the events: a sender that stayed silent produces no event, and a raster whose rows come from the event list simply loses it — along with the negative evidence that is the raster's whole point.

senderPopulationIds.description: Optional, parallel to recordedSenderIds: the population each recorded sender belongs to. The populations are exactly the distinct values here and their block order is first appearance in recordedSenderIds, so there is no second list to drift and no population id can dangle. Repetition is expected and is never treated as a duplicate-id error.

trialIds.description: The COMPLETE trial universe in display order, INCLUDING trials in which nothing happened. Required whenever eventTrialIds are supplied. Cortexel never infers the trial count from the observed trial ids: a trial with no spikes is still a trial, and it is precisely the trial you must be able to see.

window.description: The declared observation window. Half-open [start, stop) by default; an event exactly at `stop` is outside it.

timeBase.description: What t = 0 means. `absolute_clock` is the source's own clock. `trial_relative` means times were re-expressed relative to a per-trial alignment event, which Cortexel cannot verify — so it must be named, and the axis is labelled with that name rather than with a bare time.

alignmentLabel.description: Required when timeBase is trial_relative: what the times are relative to (for example "Stimulus onset"). An unlabelled relative axis is indistinguishable from an absolute one and invites the reader to date a spike to a moment that never existed.

senderUniverseComplete.description: Whether recordedSenderIds is the complete recorded universe (true) or a declared selection from a larger recording (false). This is the completeness label; it is carried into the artifact, the deterministic summary, and the table metadata.

eventCompleteness.description: An explicit assertion that every event of every declared sender inside the window is present. There is deliberately no `sampled` value: no disclosure rule exists that could mark a sampled raster honestly, and a raster whose ticks are a sample cannot be read — every gap becomes ambiguous between `did not fire` and `was not sampled`. Large rasters are handled by the count-preserving density grid instead, so caller-side event sampling is never necessary.

rowOrder.description: The Y order, and it is always explicit. There is no option to sort by an observed statistic: sorting rows by rate or first-spike latency draws a diagonal band that reads as population structure and is an artifact of the sort. To use such an order, place it in recordedSenderIds with `as_declared`, where it is recorded as your choice. `grouped_by_population` with no declared populations degenerates to a single block and is recorded as such.

markStyle.description: How each event is drawn below the mark budget. This changes only the glyph; it never changes a time, a count, or which events are drawn.

outOfWindowPolicy.description: What to do with an event outside the declared window. `reject` fails the request. `exclude_and_disclose` excludes it from the drawing, counts it, discloses the count, and keeps it in the table with inWindow = false. There is no third option in which it disappears.

aboveMarkBudget.description: What to do when the event count exceeds the visible-mark budget. `density_grid` switches to the count-preserving raster_density_bins policy and discloses it. `refuse` fails with RESOURCE_MARKS_EXCEEDED, for the case where an aggregated image would be worse than no image.

densityGrid.description: Optional explicit grid, consulted only when the mark budget is exceeded and aboveMarkBudget is density_grid. When absent the grid is derived deterministically from the declared plot geometry and the renderer revision. Either way the dimensions used are recorded in the artifact and named in the disclosure.

rowBins.description: Rows are merged only in contiguous blocks of the final row order. A tile spanning several senders is labelled as such: its count belongs to the block, not to a neuron.

accessibility.summaryTemplate: Spike raster. {eventCount} events, {excludedCount} outside the window, from {activeSenderCount} of {recordedSenderCount} recorded senders across {trialCount} trials, over {windowStart} to {windowStop} {timeUnit}, boundary {windowBoundary}, time base {timeBase}. {rowCount} rows ordered by {rowOrder}; the order is declared and is never sorted by an observed statistic. Sender universe complete: {senderUniverseComplete}. Marks drawn: {markCount}. {compactionStatement} Tick density is not a firing rate, and overlapping ticks are not separately visible; exact event times are in the table.

tableColumns.description: The event's original index in the caller's arrays. This is the audit anchor: sorting for display never changes it.
Declared id, or the deterministic ordinal identity assigned when none was declared.
The event time as supplied, after canonical unit conversion.
Empty when no trials were declared.
Empty when no populations were declared.
The row this event was drawn on, in the final row order. Rows with no events appear in the row legend, not here.
False for an event excluded by the window. Excluded events remain listed: an event removed from the table is an event nobody can find again.

externalOracle.name: nest-simulator spike_recorder
externalOracle.notes: The intended differential oracle is NEST's own spike recorder, compared fixture for fixture on the conventions that actually differ between implementations: whether an event at exactly `stop` is recorded, whether recorder output is chronological across threads and ranks, and whether a duplicate event row can occur. A secondary parity check imports the same trains through Neo SpikeTrain (t_start/t_stop) and asserts an identical accepted event set. Neither has been executed here: the pinned reference environment has not been run in this environment, so the status is not_run rather than assumed. No claim of oracle agreement may be made until it is.

adapters.notes: Events plus the RECORDED sender universe, which NEST knows (the recorded NodeCollection) and the event list does not. The adapter must not assume the recorder's `events` arrays are chronological, and must map the recorder's start/stop to the window with its endpoint convention stated rather than guessed.

One SpikeTrain per sender; t_start/t_stop map to the window and the unit is taken from the quantity, never guessed. Neo has no concept of `recorded but silent`, so the caller must supply the full sender selection explicitly — a list of SpikeTrains alone cannot produce the empty rows.

The ragged spike_times/spike_times_index pair maps to the parallel arrays, but the caller must select the unit rows explicitly. The selected rows become recordedSenderIds and senderUniverseComplete is false unless the caller selected every unit.
Not certified for 1.0. Until a mapping for the recorded-sender universe exists, an NCP payload cannot produce the empty rows and would silently become a raster of only the neurons that fired.

migration.notes: The legacy payload carried only the events that fired, with no recorded-sender universe and no explicit window. Migration preserves event identity and order and REQUIRES both `recordedSenderIds` and an observation window; without them it blocks with MIGRATION_INFORMATION_MISSING rather than inferring rows from the event list. Inferring them would delete every silent neuron from the figure — quietly turning a raster that showed 40 of 100 neurons firing into one that appears to show 40 of 40.

changePolicy: Changing the default window boundary convention from [start, stop) is MAJOR.
Changing row identity (sender versus sender x trial), or the deterministic row order produced by any `rowOrder` value, is MAJOR: the same input would draw a different figure.
Changing which compaction policy applies above the mark budget, or the default density-grid dimensions, is MAJOR for the same reason.
Allowing `eventCompleteness` to accept a sampled event set is MAJOR, and requires a disclosure rule that does not exist yet.
Removing the preservation of duplicate event rows is MAJOR and would not be done: it discards a real observation.
Adding a `markStyle` value, or an optional per-event table column, is MINOR.
Adding a row order that depends on an observed statistic is prohibited at any version.
knownLimitations: The disclosure registry has no rule keyed to `senderUniverseComplete`, so a declared sender subset appears in the deterministic summary, the artifact, and the table metadata rather than in the disclosure footer.

NODE_UNIVERSE_INCOMPLETE is the nearest existing rule to that gap, but its text is about edges and degree and would be false on a raster, so it is deliberately not emitted. A wrong disclosure is worse than a missing one.
There is likewise no disclosure id for a caller-sampled event set. Rather than emit a rule that does not fit, the contract refuses the sampled value outright.

Cortexel verifies that every event's sender is in the declared universe. It cannot verify that the recorder observed every spike of those senders, so an empty row is evidence of no RECORDED event; reading it as silence rests on the recorder, not on Cortexel.
Below the mark budget every event is drawn, but two events closer than one device pixel overlap. The table count is authoritative; the visible tick count is not, and no figure caption can make it so.

raster_density_bins preserves every event's count but not its individual identity in the drawn image, and does not preserve extrema: a single spike inside a busy tile is not distinguishable. That is exactly why COMPACTION_MAY_HIDE_TRANSIENTS is emitted alongside it.
No uncertainty variant is renderable. An event is an observation, not an estimate, and a band drawn around one would be a fabrication.

invalid.why: The recorded-sender universe is absent, so the rows can only come from the events. Here senders 1, 2 and 3 fired and sender 4 did not: inferring the rows deletes sender 4 entirely, and a raster of 4 recorded neurons of which 1 was silent becomes a raster of 3 neurons of which none was. The negative evidence — the empty row — is the one thing this figure exists to show, and it is exactly what inference destroys.

An event's sender is not in the declared recorded universe. A sender that was supposedly not recorded cannot have produced a recorded event: this is a contradiction in the source, not a value to draw on a row invented for the occasion. Silently adding row 99 would also mean the figure's sender count no longer matches the recording it claims to depict.

The event at t = 10.0 ms sits exactly on the excluded endpoint of the half-open window [0, 10). NEST's recorder can and does emit an event at exactly the stop time, so this is the common case, not a corner case. With `reject` the mismatch is surfaced; the forbidden behaviour is to clip it in silence, which makes the last spike of a train disappear and the neuron look as if it had stopped firing 1 ms before the recording ended.

Events carry trial ids but no trial universe is declared. Cortexel will not infer it from the observed ids: if 20 trials were run and 17 produced a spike, inferring the universe draws 17 rows and turns a 17-of-20 result into a 17-of-17 one. The three trials in which the neuron was silent are the strongest evidence in the dataset and they would vanish without a trace.

Sender 2 is declared twice, so the row universe is ambiguous: the figure would show 3 rows for 2 neurons, and sender 2's events would have to be assigned to one of two rows that both claim to be it. Whichever won would depend on iteration order, and a reader counting rows would over-count the population by 50%. An ambiguous identity must fail before row binding, selection, or search can diverge on it.

Three times and two senders. Pairing by best effort would leave the third spike either unowned or, worse, silently reassigned: index-aligned pairing after a dropped sender shifts every subsequent event onto the wrong row, and the raster then shows a firing pattern that no neuron in the simulation produced. Parallel arrays that describe the same observations must have identical lengths.

The caller declares that the events are a sample. There is no such value: a raster whose ticks are a subset cannot be read, because every gap is then ambiguous between `this neuron did not fire` and `this spike was not sampled`, and no legend can repair that. Cortexel handles large rasters with a count-preserving density grid instead, so sampling is never necessary — and no disclosure rule exists that could mark a sampled raster honestly.

`ms` is canonical; `milliseconds` is only an accepted alias. A stable request must use the canonical code, and the alias is rejected with a repair rather than converted in silence — a silent conversion changes numbers the caller never sees. Adapters and `cortexel migrate` may resolve an alias, because there the conversion is recorded; normal validation may not.

The event times are declared to be of kind `time` but carry a frequency unit. Cortexel will not place them on a time axis: a value of 2.0 Hz is not 2.0 ms, and drawing it at x = 2 would put a mark at a moment that was never measured while the axis label continued to claim milliseconds. A unit's dimension must match the quantity kind it is attached to.

The window is inverted, so no event can fall inside it. Accepting it would produce a raster with four empty rows and zero ticks: a picture of a completely silent network, drawn from data containing five spikes. An empty figure that looks like a measurement is the most dangerous output this library can produce, so a degenerate window fails rather than renders.

An uncertainty band is requested on a raster. A spike time is an observation, not an estimate: there is no sampling distribution over `this event happened`, and a shaded region around a tick would assert a spread that was never computed from anything. The parameters schema is closed, so the field is rejected rather than ignored — an ignored field is one the caller still believes was honoured.

The request tries to assert that Cortexel validated it. A caller declares what its data IS; it never declares what Cortexel concluded about it. Validation results, reference-comparison status, and disclosure text are library-generated facts, and a request that could set them could manufacture a figure that claims to have been checked when nothing checked it. The field is rejected, not ignored.
